references.notes: The term 'ḥarīm' refers to an inviolable sacred territory, a concept central to pre-Islamic sacred geography and tribal protection.
The verse mentions Minā and al-Muḥaṣṣab, which are sacred locations associated with the Hajj pilgrimage and pre-Islamic ritual practices.
The term 'الدهر' (Dahr) is used to denote an impersonal, destructive force of fate, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term 'الدهر' is invoked as an active agent causing events, reflecting the pre-Islamic concept of Time as an irresistible force.
The verse explicitly mentions weeping ('يبك') and lamenting ('ينْدُبُوا') for the deceased, which are key components of pre-Islamic mourning rites.
The term 'الحين' (al-ḥayn) refers to an appointed time or fate, aligning with pre-Islamic concepts of destiny and the inevitability of death.
'سنيح' (sanīḥ) explicitly refers to a type of omen (an animal passing from right to left), which is a practice of augury in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
'دوار' (Duwār) refers to a pre-Islamic idol or sacred standing stone, indicating a reference to a sacred object or space.
The verse refers to 'الدَّهْرُ' (Time/Fate) as a controlling force, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The phrase 'it was written for me in my scroll/book' implies a predetermined fate, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about destiny.
The command 'lament me' refers to the ritualistic practice of lamentation (naʿy) for the deceased, a significant pre-Islamic mourning rite.
The mention of 'true divining arrows and lots' explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic practice of divination using azlām (arrows) or aqdāḥ (lots).
The term 'المُفِيضِينَ' refers to those who cast lots or divining arrows, indicating practitioners of pre-Islamic divination.
This phrase describes the physical action of 'striking and turning in the hand' the divining arrows or lots, detailing the practice of divination.
This describes the moment when the divining arrow 'appears' or 'is revealed' after being struck, referring to the outcome of divination.
The description of the divining arrow as 'cursed' (مُلَعَّنٌ) suggests its potent, possibly taboo, or fate-revealing nature within the context of divination.
The verb 'يَقْدَحُ' (to cast lots/arrows) directly refers to the act of divination using azlām or aqdāḥ.
The term 'سِمَاكِيُّ' refers to the star al-Simāk, which was significant in pre-Islamic Arabian astral belief for weather prediction and omens.
The mention of "الظباء البوارح" (gazelles passing from right to left) refers to a specific type of augury considered a bad omen in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The poet explicitly states that Dahmāʾ is "from the jinn," directly referencing these non-divine supernatural entities in pre-Islamic belief.
The term "الدهر" (Time/Fate) is invoked as a force that causes events, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of an impersonal, governing power.
This verse explicitly mentions 'al-ṣadā' (the bone-bird), a pre-Islamic belief that the spirit of the unavenged dead takes the form of a bird crying out from the grave.
The verse refers to 'horrors from the unseen' (الغيب), which is a key cosmological concept in pre-Islamic thought, representing the unknown realm beyond human perception.
The personification of the new moon (هلال) as speaking and advising caution suggests a belief in astral omens or a form of divination based on celestial phenomena.
The mention of the hādī (الهدا), a bird often associated with ill omens or spirits of the dead in desolate places, crying out suggests a belief in augury or ominous signs.
The phrase 'نائب الدهر' explicitly refers to 'Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an active, often malevolent, cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.
This verse further personifies 'Dahr' (الدهر) as a powerful agent capable of causing corruption and misfortune, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological concepts of fate.

The phrase 'جَدُّ عَامِرٍ' (the fortune/destiny of ʿĀmir) refers to a pre-Islamic concept of inherent tribal fortune or destiny, often seen as a powerful, pervasive force, fitting into cosmological beliefs about fate.

The poem refers to 'الدَّهْر' (Time/Fate) as an active agent that brings change and affects human life, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of dahr as a powerful, often indifferent, force.

The verses explicitly mention 'القِدَاح' (lots/arrows) and 'الأَيْسَار' (lot-casters) in the context of dividing meat, referring to the pre-Islamic maysir ritual which involved a form of arrow divination for distribution.
The phrase 'شهود الجِنِّ' (witnesses of the jinn) explicitly refers to the presence or influence of jinn, a common pre-Islamic belief in non-divine supernatural entities.
The mention of 'تَمَائِمُهُ' (amulets) refers to the pre-Islamic practice of wearing charms for protection, a physical object used in a ritualistic context.
The term 'أَوْتَارِ' (vengeance/blood-feud) refers to the pre-Islamic obligation of seeking retribution for a slain kinsman, which was a quasi-ritual duty tied to tribal honor and the deceased.
The phrase 'نَوْبَةُ الدَّهْرِ' refers to the pre-Islamic concept of Dahr (Time/Fate) as an inexorable cosmological force.
The term 'على الوِتْرِ' refers to the pre-Islamic obligation of blood vengeance, a quasi-ritualistic duty to avenge the deceased.
The verse attributes agency and influence over human affairs to 'الدَّهْر' (Time/Fate), a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
This verse continues to personify 'الدَّهْر' (Time/Fate) as an unyielding and wrathful force, consistent with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs.
This verse explicitly compares the nobles to 'jinn', referring to the pre-Islamic belief in supernatural entities.
The term 'qarīna' refers to a familiar spirit or companion jinn, a concept rooted in pre-Islamic beliefs about supernatural entities.
The phrase 'تُبَشِّرُ طَيْرُهُمْ' (their birds announce) refers to the pre-Islamic practice of augury or divination from birds.
The verse explicitly mentions 'manīḥ al-qidāḥ,' referring to the arrows used for pre-Islamic divination practices.
The phrase describes people wiping the face of a revered object, a practice akin to seeking blessing from idols or sacred stones in pre-Islamic rituals.
The term 'muḥajjar' refers to a sacred or forbidden enclosure, indicating a pre-Islamic sacred space.
The verse refers to 'الدَّهْرُ' (Time/Fate) as an active force that leaves nothing but memory, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of Dahr as an impersonal agent of fate and destruction.
The term 'أَيْسَارِي' refers to the practitioners of *maysir*, a pre-Islamic Arabian ritual involving arrow divination and distribution of sacrificial meat, thus identifying a tribal sacral role.
This verse explicitly states that a rare portion of something is for 'al-Jinn', directly referencing these supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This verse explicitly mentions the slaughter or sacrifice (دبر) of a strong ram (كَبْشَةَ عِرْمِسِ), which is a common pre-Islamic ritual practice.
This verse refers to 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) as an active, inescapable force, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of destiny.
The term 'wa'y' refers to a vow or offering, implying a ritualistic act performed in times of alarm or battle, consistent with pre-Islamic practices.

These verses explicitly mention 'أَيْسَارِي' and 'قِدَاحُهُمُ' (arrows) used for determining shares of meat, a clear reference to the pre-Islamic practice of *maysir* which involved lot-casting and had ritualistic undertones.
This verse refers to the celestial bodies Mars (المِرِّيخِ) and al-Nathra (نَثْرَتَهُ), a lunar mansion, indicating pre-Islamic astral belief and cosmological understanding.
The verse refers to 'maymūn' (auspicious) and 'ash'am' (inauspicious) features of an animal and invites 'taʿayyafū' (to augur or divine), indicating a practice of seeking omens.
The verse explicitly mentions 'al-jinn' (jinn) being present and reacting with awe to the gathering, indicating a belief in these non-divine supernatural entities.
The term 'al-Manāyā' (fates/deaths) is invoked as an irresistible cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic concept of destiny.
This verse describes the sky as having pillars, which reflects a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of the heavens.
The term 'الدَّهْر' (Time/Fate) is invoked as an active, impersonal force shaping events, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verb 'تَغَوَّلَ' alludes to the 'ghūl', a type of jinn or supernatural being believed to inhabit desolate places and mislead travelers.

references.entity_or_term: نَنْحَرُهَا مَثنىً... ونُلْصِقُ بالكُوم الجِلاَدِ وقَدْ رَغَتْ أَجِنَّتُهَا
references.notes: The description of slaughtering pregnant camels in pairs, especially in times of hardship, points to a pre-Islamic sacrificial practice like al-faraʿ or al-ʿatīra.
'الدَّهْر' (Time/Fate) is again personified as an overarching force, reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in its influence over human affairs.
The verse explicitly mentions 'shayṭāna' (female demon or jinn), referring to a non-divine supernatural entity believed to influence or possess.
The phrase "بجنب السعد" refers to a location associated with Saʿd, a known pre-Islamic idol or sacred standing stone, indicating a sacred space.
The name "عبد منافة" explicitly refers to Manāfa, a pre-Islamic deity, through a theophoric personal name.
The verse explicitly names "al-Dahr" (Time/Fate) and attributes qualities to it, indicating its role as a cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.
The verse mentions "jinnān" (jinn), which are non-divine supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase "ḥaqq dahr" refers to the inevitable claim or due of "dahr" (Time/Fate), reinforcing its conceptual role as a cosmological agent.
This verse personifies "al-Dahr" (Time/Fate) as a consuming entity, reflecting a common pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of its destructive power.

This verse refers to 'al-nuḥūs' (inauspiciousness or bad luck), which in pre-Islamic belief was often attributed to the influence of stars or fate, aligning with cosmological understandings of fortune.

These verses describe an object adorned with cowrie shells (الوَدْعُ) and subjected to ritualistic care (wiping, dressing, lulling) by maidens, strongly suggesting a sacred object or idol and its associated practices.
The mention of 'obscuring of the stars' (طُمْسُ الكَوَاكِبِ) reflects a pre-Islamic belief in celestial bodies as significant agents or omens within their cosmology.
This verse refers to Dahr (Time/Fate) as an active, often malevolent, force that diminishes humans, a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
This verse further emphasizes Dahr (Time/Fate) as a powerful force that dictates fortunes, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs.
The hooting of an owl (البُومُ) is explicitly mentioned as an event that prevents guidance, reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in owls as ill omens.
This verse contains an explicit oath formula, 'By the life of your father,' which is a common pre-Islamic practice of swearing by a revered entity or person.
The verse explicitly mentions 'jinn' as a cause of ruin or madness (خبال), reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in these supernatural entities and their influence.
The term 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) is invoked as an active, separating force, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological belief in an impersonal, irresistible destiny.
The verb 'ḍaḥḥū' explicitly refers to the act of making a morning sacrifice, a common ritual practice in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The phrase describes the act of enclosing the dead in graves, which is a direct reference to pre-Islamic burial practices.
'Al-ghuyūb' (the unseen/hidden) is depicted as an active agent that 'throws' or dictates events, aligning with pre-Islamic notions of fate or unseen forces governing human affairs.
This verse explicitly mentions "lamentation" (النَّوْحِ), which is a key mourning rite in pre-Islamic Arabian mortuary practices.
The verse describes "offerings" (قَرَابِينَا) being presented to a sacred place, directly referencing sacrificial acts and votive offerings.
This verse refers to a "mourning gathering" (مَأْتَمٍ), which is a component of pre-Islamic Arabian mourning rites.
The term "الدَّهْرِ" (Time/Fate) is a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept, often seen as an impersonal force governing existence.
The mention of "قِدَاحاً" (arrows/lots) strongly alludes to the pre-Islamic practice of divination using azlām (divination arrows).
The terms "مَشَائِيمُ" (inauspicious) and "مَيَامِينَا" (auspicious) refer to concepts of fortune and omens, which are integral to pre-Islamic divination and belief.
The verb "استبهل" refers to invoking a curse or swearing a solemn oath, often with religious implications, fitting the category of oath formulae.
The term 'الدَّهْرِ' (dahr) is used here in a manner that suggests its pre-Islamic cosmological significance as an impersonal force of time or fate that governs existence.
The verse contains an oath by the father of the beloved, a common pre-Islamic oath formula invoking a sacred familial bond.
The verse explicitly mentions "jinn" (جِنّ), which are non-divine supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse explicitly names and personifies 'Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a powerful, destructive force, which was a significant pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
This verse describes a sacred place containing four idols or sacred objects made of acacia wood, indicating a pre-Islamic sanctuary or cultic site.
The verse refers to the use of two arrows, specifically 'the arrows of al-Muqaqqaʿ,' which were employed in pre-Islamic arrow divination (azlām).
The verse explicitly mentions the 'ghūl,' a type of jinn or supernatural being in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore, as a potential agent of disappearance.
The term 'al-Hiballāt' is a plural form of Hubal, a prominent pre-Islamic deity worshipped in the Kaaba.
The verse refers to 'al-ṣadā,' a pre-Islamic belief in a bird-like entity emerging from the skull of the deceased, often associated with seeking vengeance or water.
The verse explicitly states that 'jinn' inhabit a specific location, referencing these non-divine supernatural entities.
The verse uses 'ghūl' as a descriptor for a spear's blade, drawing on the terrifying and destructive nature associated with the supernatural entity.
The verse attributes agency and power to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate), which was a significant cosmological concept in pre-Islamic belief, often seen as an irresistible force.
The term 'حَرِم' (ḥaram) explicitly refers to a sacred, inviolable zone, a key concept in pre-Islamic Arabian sacred geography.
The term 'al-Ḥaram' refers to a sacred precinct or sanctuary, a key concept in pre-Islamic Arabian religious geography.
The term 'ḥarim' (حَرِم), a plural of 'ḥarīm' or 'ḥaram', can refer to a sacred enclosure or animals designated as sacred, indicating a pre-Islamic sacred space or sacrificial practice.
The term 'al-Dahr' (time/fate) is invoked, which in pre-Islamic Arabian thought often represented an impersonal, inexorable cosmological force governing existence.
This verse explicitly mentions "firākh al-hām," referring to the bird believed to emerge from the skull of the unavenged dead, a central concept in pre-Islamic mortuary belief.
This verse explicitly mentions 'al-Jinn' (jinn), referring to non-divine supernatural entities believed to inspire poets or influence events in pre-Islamic Arabian cosmology.
